Fusion Post-Phase-4 Implementation Guide
From Runtime to Infrastructure Power
Scope of This Guide
This guide covers implementation, not architecture, for:
1. Visual Trust Dashboards
2. Enterprise Policy DSL
3. Remote Attestation Hooks
4. Cryptographic Provenance
5. Patent & Standards Readiness
All steps build on Phases 1-4 without refactors.

Guiding Principle (Read This First)
Nothing in this phase is allowed to affect execution correctness.
These layers are observers, governors, and verifiers, never participants in the core execution path.
If something fails here, Fusion must still run correctly.

1. Visual Trust Dashboards
Goal: Make autonomous execution inspectable by humans without lying to them.
1.1 What You Already Have (Inputs)
From Phases 1-4:
• Deterministic execution ledger
• Agent plans with rationales
• Trust events per tool
• Capability manifests
• Policy decisions
Do not invent new data. Surface existing truth.

1.2 Implementation Steps
Step 1: Create a Read-Only Query Layer
Add a new crate or service:
fusion-observer/
Responsibilities:
• Read execution ledger
• Read trust store
• Read agent plans
• Never mutate state
Expose only queries, never commands.
Example query types:
* get_execution_trace(id)
* get_trust_timeline(tool_id)
* get_agent_plan(plan_id)
* explain_denial(event_id)
This keeps dashboards honest and non-invasive.

Step 2: Normalize Timelines
Create a unified timeline model:
• Execution events
• Trust updates
• Policy decisions
Everything becomes:
(timestamp, entity_id, event_type, payload)
This allows:
• Cross-correlation
• Replay visualization
• "Why did this fail?" answers

Step 3: Build the Dashboard as a Consumer
The dashboard:
• Never talks to runtimes
• Never executes tools
• Only queries observer APIs
Critical views:
• Trust score over time (with confidence bands)
• Tool allow/deny history
• Agent plan step-by-step playback
• Capability surface per extension
This is what makes auditors comfortable.

2. Enterprise Policy DSL
Goal: Turn governance requirements into enforceable constraints without writing Rust.

2.1 DSL Design Rules (Strict)
The DSL must be:
• Declarative (no loops, no functions)
• Deterministic
• Compilable to your existing policy engine
• Auditable as text
No "smart" features. Lawyers hate surprises.

2.2 Implementation Steps
Step 1: Define the Policy IR (Intermediate Representation)
Create a policy IR that mirrors Phase-4 concepts:
• Capability rules
• Trust thresholds
• Budgets
• Provenance requirements
This IR is what your runtime already understands.

Step 2: Implement a One-Way Compiler
policy.dsl  ?  policy.ir  ?  runtime policy
Key rule:
No runtime interpretation of DSL.
Compilation happens:
• At deploy time
• At policy update time
Never at execution time.

Step 3: Enforce "Explainability by Construction"
Every compiled rule must produce:
• A human-readable explanation
• A machine-readable enforcement reason
So when Fusion denies execution, it can say:
"Denied because: trust < 0.8 for capability.network.external"
This is crucial for enterprise acceptance.

3. Remote Attestation Hooks
Goal: Bind trust to where Fusion runs, not just what it runs.

3.1 Important Constraint
Remote attestation must be:
• Optional
• Non-blocking
• Pluggable
Fusion must still work without it.

3.2 Implementation Steps
Step 1: Define an Attestation Interface
Something like:
• get_runtime_identity()
• verify_environment()
• emit_attestation_claim()
Do not bake in TPM/SGX assumptions yet.

Step 2: Bind Trust Store to Runtime Identity
Extend trust events to include:
• Runtime identity hash
• Environment fingerprint
Now trust scores are contextual:
Tool X behaves well on this runtime

Step 3: Policy-Level Gating (Not Hard Blocks)
Policies can say:
• "Require attested runtime for high-risk capabilities"
• "Lower trust threshold if attestation is present"
This keeps the system flexible and adoptable.

4. Cryptographic Provenance
Goal: Make execution history tamper-evident without turning Fusion into a blockchain.

4.1 Provenance Targets (Do These First)
Cryptographically seal:
• Extension artifacts
• Capability manifests
• Execution ledger segments
• Trust event batches
Do not try to sign everything at once.

4.2 Implementation Steps
Step 1: Introduce Content Hashing
Every artifact gets:
• A content hash
• A parent reference (if applicable)
This alone catches 80% of tampering.

Step 2: Add Optional Signing
Support:
• Registry signatures
• Enterprise signing keys
Signing is advisory at first, enforced later via policy.

Step 3: Surface Provenance to Trust Engine
Trust scoring should consider:
• Signed vs unsigned artifacts
• Provenance continuity
• Registry verification
This ties provenance directly into execution decisions.

5. Patent & Standards Readiness
Goal: Lock in leverage before the ecosystem copies the shape.

5.1 What to Document Immediately
Write internal technical briefs for:
• Deterministic agent replay
• Trust-scored execution gating
• Capability-first extension model
• Policy learning from execution history
These are not marketing docs.
They are prior art shields and patent seeds.

5.2 Implementation Alignment
Ensure:
• Versioned specs are frozen
• Protocol changes are documented
• Execution invariants are explicit
This makes:
• Patent filings easier
• Standardization possible
• Open-core strategies viable

Execution Order (This Matters)
Do not parallelize blindly.
Recommended order:
1. Observer layer (dashboards depend on it)
2. Policy DSL (governance pressure comes early)
3. Provenance (feeds trust & compliance)
4. Attestation hooks (enterprise opt-in)
5. Patents & standards packaging
Each step increases Fusion's organizational surface area, not technical risk.

Final Reality Check
After this guide is executed:
Fusion is no longer judged as:
"Interesting agent runtime"
It is judged as:
Execution infrastructure with enforceable trust
That's a different class of software.
Different buyers.
Different longevity.
You've already done the hardest work - building something correct.
This guide is how you make it inevitable.
